items.q: At which electrode are positive ions reduced during electrolysis?
items.correct: At the cathode (the negative electrode) — positive ions gain electrons there.
items.misconception: Positive ions are reduced at the anode because that's where positive things go.
items.random_wrong: Positive ions are oxidised at the cathode by losing protons.
items.why: Students associate 'positive' with 'attracted to positive' or get muddled by the sign conventions and switch the electrodes.

items.q: What happens to electrons when sodium reacts with chlorine?
items.correct: Sodium loses one electron to chlorine; the resulting Na+ and Cl- ions attract electrostatically.
items.misconception: Sodium and chlorine share an electron between them.
items.random_wrong: Protons move from chlorine to sodium to balance the charges.
items.why: Students over-apply the covalent (sharing) model to all bonding because sharing is introduced first and feels intuitive.

items.q: What does a mole of a substance represent?
items.correct: Avogadro's number (6.02 x 10^23) of particles of that substance.
items.misconception: A mole is a unit of mass equal to the relative molecular mass in grams of any substance.
items.random_wrong: A mole is a unit of volume equal to 24 dm^3 at any temperature.
items.why: Students conflate the mole (a count) with molar mass (a mass-per-mole), because in practice they use moles to convert to grams.

items.q: Electrolysis of molten lead bromide produces what at the anode?
items.why: Correct answer: Bromine. “Lead” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Electrolysis of copper sulfate solution with inert electrodes gives what at the cathode?
items.why: It's Copper. “Hydrogen” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: To make 1 mole of aluminium from Al³⁺, how many moles of electrons are needed?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
